title: Should Public Schools Reinstate Separate Gifted and Talented Tracks in Elementary Grades?
prompt: Evaluate whether US public schools should reinstate separate gifted and talented tracks in elementary grades, reversing the post-2015 trend toward detracking and inclusion-model enrichment. Consider the research on ability grouping and achievement (Kulik, Steenbergen-Hu), the NYC and San Francisco detracking experiments, equity and demographic composition concerns, the 'excellence gap' literature, what happens to high-ability kids in detracked classrooms, teacher capacity for differentiation, and whether identification mechanisms can be made fair.

agents.bio: Educational psychologist who directs a university gifted center and has published longitudinal studies of gifted-track alumni. Cites meta-analyses showing 0.3-0.5 SD achievement gains from ability grouping with curriculum acceleration. Believes detracking is ideological malpractice that harms the very kids most likely to solve hard problems. Frustrated that her field is marginalized for producing inconvenient findings.

Sociologist of education who studies tracking, school segregation, and the reproduction of inequality. Points out that every US gifted program ever studied overrepresents white and Asian students and underrepresents Black, Latino, and low-income students relative to underlying ability distributions. Believes the 'fair identification' promise has been tried for fifty years and has never materialized. Detracking is corrective.

Veteran 4th grade teacher in a detracked district who has tried to differentiate instruction for a 25-student class spanning five grade levels of reading ability. Believes the detracking ideal assumes teacher capacity that does not exist in real schools with real class sizes. Neutral on the politics but emphatic that inclusion-model differentiation is a fiction that exhausts teachers and shortchanges both high and low performers.

Parent of two who fought the de Blasio Gifted and Talented phase-out and watched middle-class families flee to private and charter schools. Argues detracking in NYC didn't close gaps — it accelerated segregation by driving out families who could exit. The kids left behind got neither a gifted track nor the promised heterogeneous enrichment.

Psychometrician who has audited gifted identification tests including the OLSAT, NNAT, and CogAT. Finds every instrument has large SES-correlated measurement error at the 95th percentile cutoffs schools actually use. Believes any identification mechanism at age 7-8 is mostly measuring parental coaching and preschool quality, not durable ability. Reinstating tracks without solving this just reinstates the problem.
